content.full_text:
Fern Polrrrcnl PRACrrcEs CouMrssroN
428 J Street r Suite 620 r Sacramento. CA 95814-2329
(9lO 322-5660 o Fax (916) 322-0886
April 1,2009
Philip D. Kohn, City Anomey
City of lrvine
Rutan & Tucker LLP
611 Anton Blvd., Suite 1400
Costa Mesa, C492626
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our File No. I-09-029
Dear Mr. Kohn:
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict-of-interest
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1 Because your request for advice is
general in nature and does not relate to a specific governmental decision, we are treating
your request as one for informal assistance.'
Qr.rEsTroNs
1. Does Section 84308 apply to directors of the Great Park Corporation?
2. Does the $250 contribution threshold under Section 84308 apply separately to
each of an official's or candidate's committees for different offices and different election
cycles?
CONCLUSION
l. Yes. The provisions of Section 84308 apply to elected officials who are
appointed to other government agencies such as the Great Park Corporation where the
I The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Secrions 81Ofi) through 91014. All
statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair
Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections l8l l0 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California
Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2. Division 6 of the California Code of
Regulations. unless otherwise indicated.
I Informal assistance does not provide the requester with the immunity provided by an opinion or
formal written advice. (Government Code Section 831 l4; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)


File No. l-09-029
Page No. 2
members of the board of directors are not composed solely of city council members or
other members directly elected by the voters. When the Great Park Corporation directors
undertake actions involving applications for licenses, permits, or other entitlements for
use, they will be subject to Section 84308's provisions.
2. No. Section 84308 does not specify that the $250 limit applies separately to
each of a candidate's committees in federal, state, or local elections. Based on the plain
language of the statute, we conclude that under Section 84308 all contributions accepted,
solicited, or directed for one or more campaign committees of a candidate are subject to
one aggregate threshold starting at any amount over $250.
FACTS
You are the city attomey for the City of kvine (the "City"). You are requesting
informal advice on behalf of individual members of the Irvine City Council. The
councilmembers all serve as members of the board of directors of the Orange County
Great Park Corporation ("Great Park Corporation"), a nonprofit corporation that is a
"supporting organization" under Section 509(a)(3) of the United States tntemal Revenue
Code ("IRC").
The City of Irvine formed the Great Park Corporation as a nonprofit entity to
assist the city in its efforts to plan, design, construct, operate and maintain the Orange
County Great Park project ("Great Park Project"). The park is located entirely within the
City of Irvine on property owned by the city. A "supporting organization" is a type of
tax-exempt entity that supports functions of an existing government agency and serves as
a subsidiary of the govemment agency. Under the IRC a "supporting organization" must
be operated, supervised and ultimately controlled by the "supported" government agency.
You state that "the 'supported' govemment agency must possess virtual veto authority
over the'supporting organization."'
The Great Park Corporation has nine directors, with five of the nine directors
being the five elected members of the Irvine City Council and the remaining four are
"independent" directors appointed by the city council. The corporation's bylaws provide
the adoption of certain corporate matters, including all those regarding substantive
financial matters (e.g. budgets, indebtedness, expenditures and property dispositions), are
not effective unless they are approved by a majority of the members of the city council.
The city's electorate adopted an initiative ordinance, Measure R, in November
2008 for the purpose of "clarifying and restating the roles and responsibilities" of the
Great Park Corporation and the City of Irvine. The stated intent of the measure was "to
ratify and implement certain policies relating to the Orange County Great Park project
with regard to goveming structures and responsibilities of the City of lrvine and the
Orange County Great Park Corporation, funding and construction, operation and
maintenance considerations, and major policies and programs." The employees of the


File No. I-09-029
Page No. 3
Great Park Corporation are employees of the City, working as a distinct operating
department of the City under the general direction and supervision of the City Manager. -
You state that the board of directors of the Great Park Corporation "does not issue
or approve licenses, permits or entitlements for use, nor does it even possess the authority
to do so." These matters are "exclusively within the purview and authority of the Irvine
City Council." Additionally, as stated in Measure R, the "City Council reviews and has
final decision-making authority over all financial matters, specifically 'including
contracts for professional services, design, construction, operation and maintenance of
the Orange County Great Park."
During our telephone conversation of March 13, 2009, you stated that although
the Great Park Corporation does not make decisions regarding "licenses, permits or
entitlements for use," it does make recommendations and advises the Irvine City Council
regarding these proceedings, including advising on contracts involving the Great park
Project. You stated that Irvine city staff prepares a report to the city council and the
Great Park Corporation's "recommendations are embodied in the staff report." you also
stated that the board's recommendations are routinely adopted. You statid that, .'[m]ore
often than not, the recommendations of the board are accepted without substantial
modification."
You wish to know whether Section 84308 and its campaign contribution
restrictions apply to directors of the Great Park Corporation.
You also wish to know whether the $250 campaign contribution threshold under
Section 84308 can apply to each separate committee for different offices and different
election cycles that the Irvine city councilmembers may have. (For instance, if a
councilmember has campaign committees for different offices, does the $250 campaign
contribution threshold apply to each committee separately, or are contributions
aggregated for purposes of Section 84308.)
AI\ALYSIS
Section 84308
Section 84308 prohibits any officer of an agency from accepting, soliciting, or
directing a contribution of more than $250 from any party, or his or heragent, or irom
any participant, or his or her agent, while a proceeding involving a license, permit, or
other entitlement for use is pending before the agency, and for three months following the
date a final decision is rendered in the proceeding, if the officer knows or has reason to
know that the participant has a financial interest in the proceeding.
"Agency" as used in this section does not include local governmental agencies
whose members are directly elected by the voters. (Section 8a308(a)(3).) As a iesult, the


File No. l-09-029
Page No.4
provisions of Section 84308 would not apply to proceedings before the Irvine City
Council. However, Regulation 18438.1(a) further provides that officers of an agency
exempted by subdivision (aX3) of Section 84308 are exempted only when:
"(l) They are acting as members of the governing body of the agency,
and the body is acting in its entirety as itself or as the ex officio
goveming body of any other agency. This applies to a city council or
board of supervisors [that] designates itself as the redevelopment agency
for.the city or county; or
"(2) They are acting as members of any committee or subgroup of the
goveming body of the agency [that] is composed solely of members of
the governing body of the agency."
Additionally, Regulation 1 843 8. I (b) provides :
"(b) The exemption for the officers of local govemmental agencies who
are directly elected by the voters applies only to agencies whose entire
membership consists of fficers directly elected by the voters to serve
on that agency." [Emphasis added.]
Neither the exception in Regulation 18438.1(a) nor 18438.1(b) above applies to
the Great Park Corporation.
Your facts indicate that the Great Park Corporation is not composed solely of city
council members or other members directly elected by the voters. Four members of the
board of directors are appointed. Therefore, the Great Park Corporation is a "local
government agency" under Section 84308, and directors of the Great Park Colporation
are "officers" within the meaning of Section 84308. (See also Murad Advice Letter, A-
0t-267.)
Proceeding Involving a License, Permit, or Other Entitlement for Use
Section 84308's definition of a proceeding involving a "license, permit, or other
entitlement for use" includes all entitlements for use as set forth in subdivision (a)(5):
"(a) The definitions set forth in this subdivision shall govern the
interpretation of this section.
"(5) 'License, permit, or other entitlement for use' means all business,
professional, trade and land use licenses and permits and all other
entitlements for use, including all entitlements for land use, all contracts


File No. I-09-029
Page No. 5
(other than competitively bid, labor, or personal employment contracts),
and all franchises."
Therefore, decisions involving contracts and entitlements for land use fall
within 84308's definition of a proceeding involving a "license, permit, or other
entitlement for use."
You ask whether the Great Park corporation's rendering advice and
recommendations to the Irvine City Council regarding licenses, permits or other
entitlements for use may be considered a proceeding under Section 84308 even when the
corporation is not the ultimate decision maker.
The conclusion depends on whether the Great Park Corporation is "purely
advisory" or whether the corporation's advice and recommendations are regularly
approved without intervening substantive review by the ultimate decisionmaker, the
Irvine City Council.
Section 84308 and Advisory Bodies:
The prohibition under Section 84308 does not apply to an agency whose function
is purely advisory. (Larsen Advice Letter, No. A-08-2OO; Levinson Advice Letter, No.
A-83-073.) Local govemment agencies that are purely "advisory" within the meaning of.
the Act have no explicit or de facto authority to make governmental decisions. The
decisionmaking capacity possessed by a local govemmental agency can be determined
only by reference to a number of facts and circumstances.
A commission that is commonly called an "advisory" body, or one that makes
"recommendations" to an ultimate decisionmaker, does not necessarily serve an
"advisory" function within the meaning of the Act. For example, in assessing whether
members of a body established to advise a govemment agency are themselves publii
officials, the Commission analyzes whether the body's recommendations are routinely
adopted without substantive review by the ostensible decisionmaker. (See Regulation
18701(aXl).) If the recommendations are routinely adopted without substantive review,
the body's members are considered public officials under the Act because their actions
are more than purely "advisory."
The Commission has concluded that where an advisory committee makes
substantive recommendations to a governmental agency that are regularly approved
without significant modification, the committee is converted from a solely aalisory status
to one that is making or participating in the making of govemmental decisions.
(calabrese Advice Letter, I-08-067; Ball Advice Letter, No. I-g9-671.)
You state that the Great Park Corporation is advisory only and it "does not issue
or approve licenses, permits or entitlements for use, nor does it even possess the authority


File No. I-09-029
Page No. 6
to do so," as these matters are "exclusively within the purview and authority of the Irvine
City Council." In addition, you stated that the city's voters clarified and reitated the
Great Park Corporation's role by approving Measure R that states the "City Council
reviews and has final decision-making authority over all financial matters, specifically
including contracts for professional services, design, construction, operationand
maintenance of the Orange County Great Park."
In our telephone conversation of March 13,2009, you stated that the Great park
Corporation makes recommendations and advises the Irvine City Council regarding
proceedings involving licenses, permits, or other entitlements for use including advisin!
on contracts involving the Great Park Project. You stated that Irvine "city stafi pr.p*.i
a report to the city council and the Great Park Corporation's recommendationi are
embodied in the staff report." You also stated that the Great Park Corporation,s
recommendations are routinely approved. You stated that, "[m]ore often than not, the
recommendations of the board are accepted without substantial modification."
Based on the facts provided, we conclude that the Great Park Corporation is not
purely advisory in nature because its recommendations are routinely "approved without
substantial modification." Therefore, the prohibition of Section 84308(aX3) applies.
This means that Section 84308 will apply to members of the corporation's boaid of
directors if they accept, solicit, or direct contributions of more than $250 from persons
who are parties or participants in a proceeding involving a license, permit, o. oih"t
entitlement for use pending before the Great park Corporatiot.
Section 84308 and the $250 contribution threshold
You ask whether the $250 contribution threshold under Section 84308 is applied
to each of an official's or candidate's committee for elective office, or if contributions
from a source are aggregated for purposes of disclosure and disqualification.
Section 84308's provisions are unique in the Act. For example, Section 84308
has its own definition of "contribution." Under Section 84308, a "'iontribution' includes
contributions to candidates and committees in federal, state, or local elections." (Section
84308(a)(6).) Furthermore, while the normal campaign contribution limits applicable to
candidates in the Act apply on a per-election basis foieach state office the candidate
seeks (see Section 85301), Section 84308 has no such limitation. Instead, it applies to a
contribution of more than 9250 accepted, solicited, or directed by an agency ofii"e,
regardless of whether it is for himself or herself, or on behalf of any other officer,
candidate, or committee. (See Section 84308(b).) Given this broad and unique
application of the rule, and looking at the plain language of the statute, we conclude that
the $250 threshold applies to contributions from a source to all of an official's or
candidate' s committees


File No. l-O9-029
Page No. 7
Consequently, under Section 84308, all contributions accepted, solicited, or
directed for one or more campaign committees of a candidate are subject to one aggregate
threshold starting at any amount over $250.
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.
Sincerely,
Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel
By: Emelyn Rodriguez
Counsel, Legal Division
ERjgl

sections.conclusion:
l. Yes. The provisions of Section 84308 apply to elected officials who are
appointed to other government agencies such as the Great Park Corporation where the
I Informal assistance does not provide the requester with the immunity provided by an opinion or
formal written advice. (Government Code Section 831 l4; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)


File No. l-09-029
members of the board of directors are not composed solely of city council members or
other members directly elected by the voters. When the Great Park Corporation directors
undertake actions involving applications for licenses, permits, or other entitlements for
use, they will be subject to Section 84308's provisions.
2. No. Section 84308 does not specify that the $250 limit applies separately to
each of a candidate's committees in federal, state, or local elections. Based on the plain
language of the statute, we conclude that under Section 84308 all contributions accepted,
solicited, or directed for one or more campaign committees of a candidate are subject to
one aggregate threshold starting at any amount over $250.
